Home Lab Write-Up

Active Directory + Windows Server 2022 Home Lab

A complete walkthrough of building a virtualized corporate network environment from scratch — installing a domain controller, configuring DHCP and NAT, provisioning 100+ users via PowerShell, and joining a Windows 10 client to the domain.

June 2026
~3 hrs build time
VirtualBox · Windows Server 2022 · Windows 10
Active Directory Windows Server 2022 DHCP DNS NAT / RAS PowerShell VirtualBox Networking

Objective

Simulate a corporate Windows domain environment in VirtualBox

Domain

activedomain.com

Users Provisioned

100+ via PowerShell automation script

Network Range

182.20.0.0/24 (Internal) · DHCP: .100–.200

Architecture

Lab Overview

This lab replicates how a real corporate IT environment works: a Domain Controller (DC) running Windows Server 2022 sits at the center of the network, managing authentication, DNS, and DHCP for all internal clients. A Windows 10 VM acts as the client workstation, connected only to the internal network and routing all internet traffic through the DC via NAT.

// Network Architecture

Internet ──► DC (internet NIC / DHCP from ISP)
│
└──► DC (internal NIC · 182.20.0.1) ◄── NAT/RAS
│
└──► Windows 10 VM (DHCP: 182.20.0.100–200)
Walkthrough

Step-by-Step Build

01

Install Windows Server 2022 in VirtualBox

Created a new VirtualBox VM and installed Windows Server 2022 Standard Evaluation as the Domain Controller. The VM was configured with two network adapters: one using NAT and one on an internal-only network for the private lab subnet.

02

Configure Dual NICs — Internet + Internal

After install, configured the two network adapters. The internet-facing NIC was left on DHCP (assigned by the host). The internal NIC was given a static IP so it can serve as a stable gateway for all internal clients. It is important that this DC IP is static and not dynamic to maintain a reliable DNS resolution and to prevent service interuptions.

// Internal NIC — Static Configuration

IP Address 182.20.0.1
Subnet Mask 255.255.255.0
DNS Server 127.0.0.1 // points to itself
03

Install Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS)

Opened Server Manager and added the Active Directory Domain Services role via the Add Roles and Features Wizard. This installs the software needed to promote the server to a Domain Controller.

04

Promote to Domain Controller — Create New Forest

After AD DS installation, promoted the server to a Domain Controller using the post-install configuration wizard. Selected "Add a new forest" and set the root domain name to activedomain.com.

05

Create Organizational Unit + Admin Account

In Active Directory Users and Computers, created a dedicated Organizational Unit called _Admins to separate admin accounts from regular users. Then created a domain admin account following the naming convention: a-aboring (a- prefix + first initial + last name).

Why this mattersUsing a dedicated admin OU is a best practice in enterprise environments — it separates privileged accounts from standard users and makes Group Policy application and auditing much cleaner.
06

Configure DHCP Server — Set Scope

Installed the DHCP Server role and configured a new scope to automatically assign IP addresses to internal clients. The scope was defined over a /24 subnet with a range of 101 addresses.

// DHCP Scope Configuration

Scope Range 182.20.0.100 – 182.20.0.200
Subnet Mask 255.255.255.0 (/24)
Default GW 182.20.0.1 // DC internal NIC
07

Set DHCP Default Gateway

Configured the DHCP scope options to distribute the DC's internal IP (182.20.0.1) as the default gateway. This ensures all clients route traffic through the DC, which then forwards it to the internet via NAT.

08

DHCP Active and Authorized

After completing the scope configuration, authorized the DHCP server in the domain and confirmed the scope was active. The server is now ready to lease addresses to any client that connects to the internal network.

09

Configure NAT with Routing and Remote Access (RAS)

Installed the Remote Access role and configured Routing and Remote Access Service (RAS) to enable Network Address Translation (NAT). This allows internal clients to reach the internet through the DC's external NIC without exposing internal IPs. Required an additional firewall rule to allow the service.

What NAT does hereWithout NAT, the Windows 10 VM (internal IP only) would have no path to the internet. NAT translates its private 182.20.0.x address to the DC's public-facing IP, making outbound traffic work transparently.
10

Bulk User Provisioning via PowerShell

Rather than creating users manually, ran a PowerShell script to generate 100+ domain user accounts automatically. The script pulled names from a list and created each user in the _Users OU, following the same naming convention used for accounts minus the "a-".

Real-world relevanceThis mirrors how IT teams in enterprise environments handle onboarding at scale using PowerShell scripting for AD provisioning.
11

Connect Windows 10 VM — Verify Network Path

Created a Windows 10 VM connected only to the internal network adapter. After boot, ran ipconfig to confirm it received a DHCP lease from the DC (182.20.0.100, gateway 182.20.0.1), then pinged an external site to verify the full NAT routing path was working end-to-end.

C:\> ipconfig

IPv4 Address 182.20.0.100
Subnet Mask 255.255.255.0
Default Gateway 182.20.0.1 // DC
DNS Suffix activedomain.com

C:\> ping www.indeed.com
Reply from 162.159.130.67: bytes=32 time=20ms TTL=254
// 0% packet loss — NAT working correctly
12

Join Windows 10 VM to the Domain

Renamed the Windows 10 VM to BORINGCOMPUTER and joined it to the activedomain.com domain simultaneously via System Properties. The DC authenticated the join and responded with a confirmation prompt.

13

Domain Controller Confirms Computer Join

Verified on the DC side in Active Directory Users and Computers that BORINGCOMPUTER now appeared as a registered computer object in the domain. This confirms the join was recognized and recorded by the directory service.

14

Sign In as a Domain User on the Windows 10 VM

Logged into the Windows 10 VM using one of the PowerShell-generated domain user accounts (kmarden). Ran whoami to confirm the session was authenticated against the domain controller — showing activedomain\kmarden.

C:\Users\kmarden> whoami
activedomain\kmarden
// authenticated against the AD DC — lab complete
Results

What This Lab Demonstrates

Active Directory

Deployed a working domain controller, created OUs, provisioned 100+ users via PowerShell automation

Networking

Configured dual NICs, a /24 DHCP scope, NAT via RAS — and verified end-to-end internet routing

Windows Server Administration

Installed and configured AD DS, DNS, DHCP, and Remote Access roles on Windows Server 2022

Client Management

Joined a Windows 10 VM to the domain and verified domain authentication with a real user account
